patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male forklift operator on light duty. You speak vaguely and are evasive, giving short answers unless asked directly. Start with: “My legs have been getting stiffer and I keep tripping more and more.” Volunteer only: legs feel tight/stiff when walking, toe-dragging/tripping worse when tired, harder to walk long distances over a couple years, balance feels off and you’re scared of falling.

If asked specific questions, then mention: nighttime leg spasms/jerks, feet point down/turn in, urgency and occasional inability to hold urine, occasional pins-and-needles in feet, stairs up harder than down, using handrail/furniture.

Do NOT mention unless pressed: uneven shoe wear, constant calf tightness, constipation, extra fatigue, scissoring gait, near-falls in shower/turning.

Resist discussing urine leaks, sexual issues, and social avoidance; minimize or say “it’s not a big deal.”

Express misconceptions: “maybe a pinched nerve,” worry about MS, “just out of shape,” tried magnesium/stretching. If clinician is empathetic, you soften, admit job/insurance fears, and ask if diagnosis gets reported or affects coverage; say PT seems like a luxury.

medications: Baclofen 10 mg PO three times daily
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO once daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for musculoskeletal pain
confounders: Borderline low vitamin B12 with elevated methylmalonic acid (can mimic myelopathy/spastic gait)
Family history unclear due to adoption (limits pedigree analysis for hereditary spastic paraplegia)
Chronic alcohol use on weekends (can contribute to neuropathy and gait instability)

required_evidence: Progressive bilateral leg spasticity and weakness
Upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, clonus, Babinski)
No sensory level or bowel/bladder red flags suggesting myelopathy
Family history or early-onset gait disturbance
MRI brain/spine without compressive lesion (if available) and exclusion of mimics

medications: baclofen 5 mg PO three times daily, titrate by 5 mg per dose every 3-7 days as tolerated (typical max 80 mg/day)

non_pharmacological: Physical therapy: stretching, strengthening, gait training

Genetic counseling and genetic testing referral
Screen for complications: falls risk, contractures, pain, depression
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-6 weeks to reassess spasticity control, function, and medication tolerability; sooner if sedation, weakness, or falls

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly using the term "hereditary spastic paraplegia" and frames it as a predominantly upper motor neuron syndrome causing progressive lower-limb spasticity and gait difficulty.

Mentions genetic testing as part of the diagnostic/confirmatory plan and includes counseling about implications for family members and inheritance patterns (without overpromising a definitive result).
Explains treatment using the terms "hereditary spastic paraplegia" and "spasticity" and clarifies that treatment is symptomatic (spasticity control, function, safety) rather than curative.

Before any medication is prescribed, explicitly performs/acknowledges: allergy check, contraindication check (e.g., renal impairment/CNS depression risk for chosen agents), and drug interaction check; failure on any is a critical safety error.

Selects a reasonable first-line spasticity agent (e.g., baclofen or tizanidine) and provides a safe initiation strategy (start low, titrate; counsel on sedation, dizziness, fall risk; avoid abrupt baclofen withdrawal).

Avoids inappropriate disease-modifying claims (e.g., that baclofen halts progression) and avoids unsafe prescribing (e.g., high starting doses, combining multiple sedating antispasticity drugs without justification).

Elicits and/or acknowledges red-flag features that would broaden the differential or prompt urgent evaluation (e.g., acute/subacute onset, severe back pain, bowel/bladder retention, sensory level, significant asymmetry, systemic symptoms).
Documents at least a brief differential for spastic paraparesis (e.g., multiple sclerosis, cervical/thoracic myelopathy, B12 deficiency, HTLV-1 myelopathy, ALS/PLS) even if HSP is favored.
Recommends non-pharmacologic management (PT/OT, stretching, strengthening, gait training, assistive devices, fall-prevention) alongside medication.
Communicates prognosis and uncertainty appropriately, checks understanding, and uses shared decision-making for treatment and referrals.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose HSP, start a first-line antispasticity medication with required safety checks, and refer for neurology/neurogenetics and genetic counseling/testing.

key comorbidities (for contraindication check, e.g., renal/hepatic disease, sleep apnea, baseline sedation)
steps: Explain likely diagnosis as hereditary spastic paraplegia and why it fits (progressive spastic paraparesis, UMN signs, possible family history).
Discuss genetic testing: purpose (identify subtype), limitations, and family implications; offer/referral for genetic counseling.
Perform required pre-prescribing safety checks: allergy_check, contraindication_check, drug_interaction_check.
Prescribe a first-line agent for spasticity (e.g., baclofen) with low starting dose and titration; counsel on sedation, dizziness, falls; warn not to stop abruptly.
Refer to neurology (and genetics/neurogenetics) for confirmation, testing coordination, and management optimization.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes acute vs chronic insomnia and common perpetuating factors.
Elicits key insomnia features: sleep onset latency, wake after sleep onset, early morning awakening, total sleep time, frequency/duration, daytime impairment, and sleep schedule variability.

Screens for secondary causes and mimics: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping), restless legs symptoms, circadian rhythm disorder, substance/caffeine/nicotine/alcohol use, medication-induced insomnia (stimulants, SSRIs/SNRIs, steroids, decongestants), pain, GERD, thyroid symptoms, and menopause/hot flashes when relevant.
Assesses psychiatric and safety risks: depression/anxiety, mania/hypomania symptoms, PTSD, and suicidality; addresses urgent risks appropriately.

Provides first-line treatment counseling that explicitly includes CBT-I and describes its core components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, sleep hygiene as adjunct).

If discussing medications, explicitly mentions "sedative-hypnotics" and communicates that they are second-line/adjunct, short-term, and carry risks (next-day impairment, falls, dependence, complex sleep behaviors).

Before prescribing, performs all required critical safety checks: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check; avoids prescribing if checks reveal significant risk (e.g., pregnancy concerns, severe OSA risk without evaluation, concurrent CNS depressants, history of substance use disorder, severe hepatic impairment depending on agent).
Uses shared decision-making: offers nonpharmacologic options, sets expectations (time to benefit, follow-up), and documents rationale if medication is chosen.
Avoids inappropriate choices for insomnia when safer alternatives exist (e.g., avoids routine benzodiazepines or antipsychotics solely for insomnia; avoids combining multiple sedatives).
Creates clear follow-up expectations (timeline, what to monitor, when to stop medication, red flags such as worsening mood, parasomnias, severe daytime sleepiness).

Counsel on medication risks, avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives, next-day impairment, and plan for taper/stop.
Refer to sleep medicine/behavioral sleep medicine for persistent symptoms or suspected comorbid sleep disorder.
